content.text: Why PRD Approval Gates Exist for Claude Features

Standard software PRDs typically have one or two review cycles — a product review and an engineering review. Claude features require a four-gate process because they span four distinct risk domains: completeness (all required sections present), behaviour specification correctness (must/must-not items are testable), eval plan readiness (every acceptance criterion has a test), and risk/ethics (failure modes are mitigated and data handling is documented).

A PRD approval gate is not a bureaucratic checkpoint — it is the last moment to catch a design flaw before it becomes an implementation that is expensive to change. The earlier a gap is caught, the cheaper it is to fix.

Each gate has a specific owner and a specific scope. Combining them into a single 'approval meeting' defeats the purpose: Legal cannot assess whether must/must-not items are testable, and the AI/ML lead cannot assess regulatory data handling requirements. The gates must be sequential and owned separately.

attrs.alt: PRD Review and Approval Gate Process showing four sequential gates (Completeness, Behaviour Spec, Eval Plan, Risk/Ethics) with PASS and FAIL paths, gate criteria table with owners and SLAs

content.text: The gate process is sequential — a PRD cannot enter Gate 2 until Gate 1 passes. This prevents reviewers from wasting time on spec quality (Gate 2) when required sections are missing (Gate 1). Gate 4 requires sign-off from Security, Legal, and an executive sponsor because it has external liability implications.
Running Gate Checks with Structured Tool Use

For teams reviewing multiple PRDs in parallel, running gates manually is slow. The following utility automates the four gate checks using Claude as a structured reviewer. Each gate check returns blocking issues and advisory notes in a machine-readable format that feeds into a PR comment or a CI report.

Gate 2 and Gate 4 require review from multiple stakeholders with different expertise. In practice, reviewers are asynchronous — Legal reviews on Tuesday, Security on Thursday. The following pattern simulates structured reviews from each role in parallel and consolidates their comments into a single report.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

REVIEW_COMMENT_TOOL = {
    "name": "add_review_comment",
    "description": "Add a structured review comment to a PRD section.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "section":    {"type": "string"},
            "severity":   {"type": "string", "enum": ["blocking","advisory","nit"]},
            "comment":    {"type": "string"},
            "suggestion": {"type": ["string", "null"]},
            "resolved":   {"type": "boolean"}
        },
        "required": ["section","severity","comment","resolved"]
    }
}

def generate_reviewer_comments(
    reviewer_role: str,
    prd_text: str,
    focus_sections: list[str]
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Simulate a structured review from a specific reviewer role.
    Each reviewer gets a different system prompt that scopes their focus.
    Returns structured review comments.
    """
    role_prompts = {
        "ai_ml_lead": (
            "You are the AI/ML lead reviewing a PRD for a Claude-powered feature. "
            "Focus on: system prompt correctness, model configuration rationale, "
            "whether the behaviour spec items are technically achievable, "
            "and whether the eval plan will actually detect the failure modes."
        ),
        "legal": (
            "You are a legal reviewer examining a PRD for a Claude-powered feature. "
            "Focus on: whether the non-negotiable constraints adequately prevent "
            "binding advice, whether data handling is compliant with your jurisdiction, "
            "and whether the failure mode mitigations address legal risk."
        ),
        "security": (
            "You are a security engineer reviewing a PRD for a Claude-powered feature. "
            "Focus on: prompt injection risks, data exfiltration via the model, "
            "whether the scope classifier adequately handles adversarial inputs, "
            "and whether the failure mode registry covers security failure modes."
        ),
    }

    system_prompt = role_prompts.get(reviewer_role, "You are a PRD reviewer.")

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1500,
        system=system_prompt,
        tools=[REVIEW_COMMENT_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Review the following PRD sections: {focus_sections}\n\n"
                f"{prd_text[:2000]}\n\n"
                "Add a review comment (blocking, advisory, or nit) for every issue you find. "
                "Include a concrete suggestion for each blocking comment."
            )
        }]
    )
    return [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "add_review_comment"]

def run_parallel_review(prd_text: str) -> dict:
    """Run all reviewer roles and consolidate their comments."""
    all_comments = []
    roles = ["ai_ml_lead", "legal", "security"]
    for role in roles:
        comments = generate_reviewer_comments(role, prd_text, ["gate2","gate4"])
        for c in comments:
            c["reviewer"] = role
        all_comments.extend(comments)

    blocking = [c for c in all_comments if c["severity"] == "blocking"]
    return {
        "total_comments": len(all_comments),
        "blocking": len(blocking),
        "advisory": len([c for c in all_comments if c["severity"] == "advisory"]),
        "gate_passed": len(blocking) == 0,
        "comments": all_comments,
    }

Simulated reviews are not a replacement for human review — they are a pre-flight check that ensures obvious issues are caught before human reviewers spend time on the PRD. A simulated review that returns zero blocking issues saves 30–60 minutes of human reviewer time; one that returns five blocking issues prevents the PRD from entering the human review queue prematurely.

Every PRD should be stored as a structured JSON file in the repository and validated by a CI gate on every pull request. The CI gate runs all four gate checks programmatically and fails the PR if any blocking criteria are not met.

The CI gate catches structural and completeness issues automatically. It does not catch semantic issues — a must/must-not item that is syntactically correct but logically contradictory still requires human review. The gate reduces the surface area that human reviewers must cover and ensures no PRD reaches human review with missing sections or empty fields.

Each gate has a service level agreement that defines the maximum time before a reviewer must respond. If a reviewer misses the SLA, the gate is escalated to their manager. The SLAs below are illustrative — adapt them to your organisation's review cadence:
Escalate to CEO for regulated industries

Escalation should be automatic — a Slack notification or JIRA escalation triggered by a calendar check, not a manual reminder. Missed gate SLAs are a leading indicator of PRD review capacity problems; track them as an engineering metric alongside cycle time.
A PRD approval is not permanent. Four events require a gate re-review:
: any change to the system prompt must re-run Gate 2 (behaviour spec review) and Gate 3 (eval plan review). The prompt hash in the CI gate will detect unauthorized changes.
New failure mode discovered in production
: add the failure mode to the registry and re-run Gate 4. Critical/High failure modes without mitigations block further feature deployment.
: upgrading the model version requires re-running the full eval suite (Gate 3 SLA) and re-reviewing the system prompt against the new model's behaviour (Gate 2 SLA).
: if the target persona is updated (e.g. a new feature is extended to a zero-tolerance persona), re-run Gates 2 and 4 — the severity calibration of all failure modes changes.

Changes that do not require a gate re-review: UI copy changes that do not affect the Claude interaction, backend infrastructure changes that do not affect the prompt or context, performance optimisations that do not change model parameters or prompt content.

PRD approval gates for Claude features enforce a four-stage review process that separates completeness (Gate 1), behaviour specification quality (Gate 2), eval plan readiness (Gate 3), and risk/ethics clearance (Gate 4). Automated CI gates catch structural issues before human review. Parallel reviewer simulation surfaces semantic issues before human reviewers spend time on the PRD. Gate re-reviews are triggered by system prompt changes, new production failure modes, model version upgrades, and persona dimension changes. Together, the gate process ensures that every Claude feature is built from a specification that is complete, testable, evaluated, and risk-cleared before implementation begins.
